response_text:
## Comparing Floating Point Numbers with `==` in IEEE 754

The short answer is: **it depends on the situation**.

---

## When `==` is Safe

### 1. Comparing with Zero (Sometimes)
```c
float x = 0.0f;
if (x == 0.0f) { ... }  // Safe IF x was explicitly assigned 0
```

### 2. Exact Integer Values (Within Precision)
```c
float x = 2.0f;
if (x == 2.0f) { ... }  // Safe - powers of 2 are exact
```

### 3. Checking if Two Variables Share the Same Value
```c
if (a == b) { ... }  // Safe if both came from the same source
```

### 4. Special Values
```c
// NaN - NEVER equal to anything, including itself
double nan = NaN;
nan == nan;  // Always FALSE - use isnan() instead

// Infinity - well defined
INFINITY == INFINITY;  // TRUE
```

---

## When `==` is Unsafe

### The Core Problem: Rounding Errors
```c
double x = 0.1 + 0.2;
x == 0.3;  // FALSE! x is 0.30000000000000004
```

### Accumulated Errors
```c
double sum = 0.0;
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) sum += 0.1;
sum == 1.0;  // FALSE
```

---

## Safe Alternatives

### Absolute Epsilon (for values near zero)
```c
bool nearly_equal(double a, double b) {
    return fabs(a - b) < 1e-9;
}
```

### Relative Epsilon (for large values)
```c
bool nearly_equal(double a, double b, double epsilon) {
    return fabs(a - b) <= epsilon * fmax(fabs(a), fabs(b));
}
```

```c
bool nearly_equal(double a, double b, double epsilon) {
